result.markdown:
--- title: "A public rejection of OMB's proposed grant rule" description: | OMB has proposed a sweeping rewrite of the rules that govern every federal grant. Thousands of people filed comments. I downloaded them to look at what they actually say. date: '2026-07-18' image: "stance.png" categories: - R - data - policy - science format: html draft: true echo: false --- In May 2026, the White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB) quietly proposed one of the most consequential changes to federal research funding in a generation. It's a rule with a boring name: the [*Regulation for Federal Financial Assistance*](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/05/29/2026-10817/regulation-for-federal-financial-assistance). The vast majority of people in their normal lives will never directly encounter this rule, but if passed as it's currently written, it will undoubtedly impact everyone one way or another. The rule fundamentally undermines science by putting political appointees--not experts--in charge of all federal grants, including the National Science Foundation (NSF), the National Institutes of Health (NIH), all federal departments (Dept. of Defense, Dept. of Energy, etc.) - every single grant by any federal agency. *Science* Editor-in-Chief Holden Thorp wrote in a June editorial titled ["Another red alert for American science,"](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.aej3572) that the proposed rule would bypass congressional intent and starve federal research. The [rule does far more](https://defendfederalresearch.org/) than that, but that at least gives you a sense of the scope. This post is a summary of what people said in the public comment period from May 29 to July 13, 2026. The code for the complete anlaysis is available at [https://github.com/jhelvy/omb-grant-rule-comments](https://github.com/jhelvy/omb-grant-rule-comments). **TLDR**: The response by the public was a **resounding opposition to the rule change**. ## An historic response Despite OMB's effort to minimize public comments on the ruling (allocating just 45 days for public comment on a ruling that affects over $1 trillion in federal assistance), the comment period drew a staggering response. The total number of comments is unclear due to how comments are actually counted (see [this post](https://presentofcoding.substack.com/p/no-there-arent-500000-comments-on) as an example of how you can inflate the official count, but you can basically just say that one comments represents a lot of people, so 1 comment can be counted as 1 million...yet it's that dumb), but the official count is 496,775 comments.
::: {.aside} Image from [post by Christopher Steven Marcum](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7482810379866271744/) ::: Now, as I said above, that number is almost certainly highly inflated, but the response is still historic by any measure. As of writing this on 07/18/26, **~54,000 individual comments** have been released by OMB, which includes all comments through the end of June. Since the comment period went through July 13, **I expect the total comment number to grow to somewhere between 70k and 80k**, give or take 10k. As [noted by Christopher Steven Marcum](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7482810379866271744/), that number is completely unprecedented in the 13-year-history of 2 CFR 200 public comments, which previously drew just 300, 215, and 2,008 comments in 2013, 2019, and 2023, respectively. ::: {.callout-note} OMB reviews and releases comments based on the date recieved, so once all the comments are released, I will update this post with a complete analysis. ::: ## What the rule actually does The proposed rule rewrites [2 CFR Part 200](https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-2/subtitle-A/chapter-II/part-200) --- the "Uniform Guidance" that sets the ground rules for federal grants --- and the single biggest move is a change in *status*: it would convert what has long been non-binding **guidance** into binding **regulation**. On top of thatreclassification, it layers in a set of substantive changes that lawyers have written up in detail ([Holland & Knight](https://www.hklaw.com/en/insights/publications/2026/06/omb-rule-proposes-significant-changes-to-federal-financial-assistance), [Faegre Drinker](https://www.faegredrinker.com/en/insights/publications/2026/6mb-proposes-extensive-reformation-of-federal-grant-regulations)). A few of the ones that show up over and over in the comments: - **Political review of grants before they're awarded.** Under the proposed §200.205, a senior political appointee would review and could override the merit / peer-review process that agencies like NIH and NSF use to decide which science gets funded. - **Discretionary termination "in the national interest."** The proposed §200.340 would let an agency terminate a grant, in whole or in part, whenever it decides the award no longer serves "agency priorities" or "the national interest." Yes, the language is actually that vague (on purpose). - **New limits on what grant money can pay for** --- publication and open-access fees (§200.461), conference attendance (§200.432), professional memberships (§200.454) --- plus restrictions on foreign collaborations (§200.220) and a "domestic-first" framework for R&D. If any of that sounds like it would reshape how research actually gets done in this country, well, that's more or less what the commenters think too. ## A quick note on the numbers Here's the thing you have to keep in mind for everything that follows. The docket page reports **hundreds of thousands** of comments *received*, but regulations.gov only posts a fraction of them individually, and it releases them in **batches** over time. So the comments you can actually download are a *moving, partial* slice of the whole. As I write this I've pulled **53,991** individually-posted comments, and that number is still climbing as more get released.[The full pipeline --- scraping, consolidating, and this analysis --- is on [GitHub](https://github.com/jhelvy/omb-grant-rule-comments) and is fully reproducible.]{.aside} So treat all of this as a **snapshot**, not a final tally. I'll re-run it once the dust settles. ## Overwhelmingly opposed The headline is not subtle. Of the 53,991 comments I've analyzed so far, roughly **92% read as opposed** to the rule, versus under **5.3% in support**. The rest didn't land cleanly on one side of my (admittedly crude) classifier.
I should be honest about how I got that number, because it matters.[This is exactly the kind of task a large language model would do far better than keyword matching --- reading each comment and judging its stance. That's my planned next step; consider these counts the fast first pass.]{.aside} I did *not* read 53,991 comments. I scored stance with a keyword heuristic: high-precision phrases that name the rule ("oppose this rule," "urge you to withdraw"), backed up by a net count of negative-vs-positive language for the comments that don't use an obvious phrase. It's approximate. It will misclassify some comments, and it leaves a chunk in the "unclear" bucket (mostly short or rhetorically indirect comments). But the direction is unmistakable, and spot-checking the "unclear" pile turns up far more quiet opposition than genuine neutrality --- so if anything, this *understates* how lopsided the response is. ## Which parts of the rule people are reacting to Only about one in six comments cites a specific section number, but the ones that do point in a clear direction. Here's how often each section of the rule gets named:
The top two are the ones you'd expect from the summaries above: **§200.205** (political review of merit review, cited in 14% of comments) and **§200.340** (termination authority, 12%). But look at what comes next --- a tight cluster of *cost* sections: publication and open-access fees (§200.461), conference costs (§200.432), and professional memberships (§200.454). Those three get cited together, at nearly identical rates, far more than you'd expect from people writing independently. That's the fingerprint of **organized comment campaigns** circulating shared templates that call out the same specific provisions --- something worth digging into on its own. ## And the themes they raise Zooming out from section numbers to themes (here I'm matching topic keywords, so a single comment can hit several), the same story shows up in plainer language:
The dominant concern, by a wide margin, is **political interference in the review of grants** (63% of comments), followed by the broader **impact on research and science funding** (38%) and the fate of **merit and peer review** (32%). Termination authority (25%) rounds out the top tier. The through-line across all of it is a fear that funding decisions currently made on scientific merit would become subject to political discretion. ## What people are saying elsewhere ::: {.callout-note} This section is a placeholder --- I'll add the outside commentary I've been collecting and tie it back to the patterns in the data. ::: ## Caveats, and what's next Let me restate the health warnings, because they're important: 1. **This is a partial, moving snapshot.** Only a fraction of the comments received have been individually posted, and they're still coming in. These numbers will shift. 2. **Stance is a keyword heuristic, not a careful read.** It's good enough to see the overwhelming shape of the response, but not for precise percentages. Running an LLM over the full text of each comment is the obvious upgrade, and it's next on my list. 3. **Everything here is reproducible.** The scraping and analysis code, along with the consolidated data, live in [this repo](https://github.com/jhelvy/omb-grant-rule-comments). If you want to check my work or slice it differently, please do. Even with all those caveats, the picture is clear enough to state plainly: the public comments on this rule are running heavily against it, they're focused on the provisions that would inject political review into the funding of science, and a meaningful share of them are coordinated. I'll update this once the full set of comments is available --- and once I've replaced my keyword guesses with something that actually reads.
